scene_title: London. The Presence chamber in the King's palace

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Where is my gracious Lord of Canterbury?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Not here in presence.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Send for him, good uncle.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Shall we call in the ambassador, my liege?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Not yet, my cousin: we would be resolved,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Before we hear him, of some things of weight

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That task our thoughts, concerning us and France.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: God and his angels guard your sacred throne

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And make you long become it!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Sure, we thank you.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My learned lord, we pray you to proceed

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And justly and religiously unfold

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why the law Salique that they have in France

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or should, or should not, bar us in our claim:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And God forbid, my dear and faithful lord,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That you should fashion, wrest, or bow your reading,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or nicely charge your understanding soul

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With opening titles miscreate, whose right

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Suits not in native colours with the truth;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For God doth know how many now in health

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall drop their blood in approbation

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Of what your reverence shall incite us to.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore take heed how you impawn our person,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How you awake our sleeping sword of war:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We charge you, in the name of God, take heed;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For never two such kingdoms did contend

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Without much fall of blood; whose guiltless drops

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Are every one a woe, a sore complaint

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: ’Gainst him whose wrongs gives edge unto the swords

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That makes such waste in brief mortality.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Under this conjuration speak, my lord;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For we will hear, note and believe in heart

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That what you speak is in your conscience wash’d

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As pure as sin with baptism.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Then hear me, gracious sovereign, and you peers,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: That owe yourselves, your lives and services

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To this imperial throne. There is no bar

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To make against your highness’ claim to France

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: But this, which they produce from Pharamond,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: “In terram Salicam mulieres ne succedant:”

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: “No woman shall succeed in Salique land:”

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which Salique land the French unjustly glose

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To be the realm of France, and Pharamond

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The founder of this law and female bar.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Yet their own authors faithfully affirm

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: That the land Salique is in Germany,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Between the floods of Sala and of Elbe;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Where Charles the Great, having subdued the Saxons,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: There left behind and settled certain French;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Who, holding in disdain the German women

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: For some dishonest manners of their life,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Establish’d then this law; to wit, no female

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Should be inheritrix in Salique land:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which Salique, as I said, ’twixt Elbe and Sala,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Is at this day in Germany call’d Meissen.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Then doth it well appear the Salique law

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Was not devised for the realm of France;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Nor did the French possess the Salique land

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Until four hundred one and twenty years

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: After defunction of King Pharamond,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Idly supposed the founder of this law;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Who died within the year of our redemption

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Four hundred twenty-six; and Charles the Great

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Subdued the Saxons, and did seat the French

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Beyond the river Sala, in the year

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Eight hundred five. Besides, their writers say,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: King Pepin, which deposed Childeric,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Did, as heir general, being descended

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of Blithild, which was daughter to King Clothair,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Make claim and title to the crown of France.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Hugh Capet also, who usurp’d the crown

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of Charles the duke of Lorraine, sole heir male

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of the true line and stock of Charles the Great,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To find his title with some shows of truth,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Though, in pure truth, it was corrupt and naught,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Convey’d himself as the heir to the Lady Lingare,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Daughter to Charlemain, who was the son

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To Lewis the emperor, and Lewis the son

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Of Charles the Great. Also King Lewis the Tenth,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Who was sole heir to the usurper Capet,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Could not keep quiet in his conscience,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Wearing the crown of France, till satisfied

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: That fair Queen Isabel, his grandmother,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Was lineal of the Lady Ermengare,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Daughter to Charles the foresaid duke of Lorraine:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: By the which marriage the line of Charles the Great

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Was reunited to the crown of France.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: So that, as clear as is the summer’s sun,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: King Pepin’s title and Hugh Capet’s claim,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: King Lewis his satisfaction, all appear

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To hold in right and title of the female:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: So do the kings of France unto this day;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Howbeit they would hold up this Salique law

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To bar your highness claiming from the female,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And rather choose to hide them in a net

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Than amply to imbar their crooked titles

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Usurp’d from you and your progenitors.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: May I with right and conscience make this claim?

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The sin upon my head, dread sovereign!

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: For in the Book of Numbers is it writ,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: When the man dies, let the inheritance

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Descend unto the daughter. Gracious lord,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Stand for your own; unwind your bloody flag;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Look back into your mighty ancestors:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Go, my dread lord, to your great-grandsire’s tomb,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: From whom you claim; invoke his warlike spirit,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And your great-uncle’s, Edward the Black Prince,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Who on the French ground play’d a tragedy,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Making defeat on the full power of France,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Whiles his most mighty father on a hill

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Stood smiling to behold his lion’s whelp

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Forage in blood of French nobility.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: O noble English, that could entertain

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: With half their forces the full pride of France

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And let another half stand laughing by,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: All out of work and cold for action!

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Awake remembrance of these valiant dead

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: And with your puissant arm renew their feats:

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: You are their heir; you sit upon their throne;

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: The blood and courage that renowned them

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Runs in your veins; and my thrice-puissant liege

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Is in the very May-morn of his youth,

lines.speaker: ELY
lines.text: Ripe for exploits and mighty enterprises.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Your brother kings and monarchs of the earth

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Do all expect that you should rouse yourself,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: As did the former lions of your blood.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: They know your grace hath cause and means and might;

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: So hath your highness; never king of England

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Had nobles richer and more loyal subjects,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Whose hearts have left their bodies here in England

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: And lie pavilion’d in the fields of France.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: O, let their bodies follow, my dear liege,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: With blood and sword and fire to win your right;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: In aid whereof we of the spiritualty

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Will raise your highness such a mighty sum

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As never did the clergy at one time

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Bring in to any of your ancestors.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We must not only arm to invade the French,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But lay down our proportions to defend

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Against the Scot, who will make road upon us

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With all advantages.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: They of those marches, gracious sovereign,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Shall be a wall sufficient to defend

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Our inland from the pilfering borderers.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We do not mean the coursing snatchers only,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But fear the main intendment of the Scot,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who hath been still a giddy neighbour to us;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For you shall read that my great-grandfather

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Never went with his forces into France

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But that the Scot on his unfurnish’d kingdom

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Came pouring, like the tide into a breach,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With ample and brim fullness of his force,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Galling the gleaned land with hot assays,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Girdling with grievous siege castles and towns;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That England, being empty of defence,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hath shook and trembled at the ill neighbourhood.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: She hath been then more fear’d than harm’d, my liege;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: For hear her but exampled by herself:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: When all her chivalry hath been in France

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And she a mourning widow of her nobles,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: She hath herself not only well defended

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: But taken and impounded as a stray

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The King of Scots; whom she did send to France,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To fill King Edward’s fame with prisoner kings

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And make her chronicle as rich with praise

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As is the ooze and bottom of the sea

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: With sunken wreck and sumless treasuries.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: But there’s a saying very old and true,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: “If that you will France win,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Then with Scotland first begin:”

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: For once the eagle England being in prey,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: To her unguarded nest the weasel Scot

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Comes sneaking and so sucks her princely eggs,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Playing the mouse in absence of the cat,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: To tear and havoc more than she can eat.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: It follows then the cat must stay at home:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Yet that is but a crush’d necessity,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Since we have locks to safeguard necessaries,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And pretty traps to catch the petty thieves.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: While that the armed hand doth fight abroad,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The advised head defends itself at home;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: For government, though high and low and lower,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Put into parts, doth keep in one consent,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Congreeing in a full and natural close,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Like music.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Therefore doth heaven divide

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The state of man in divers functions,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Setting endeavour in continual motion;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To which is fixed, as an aim or butt,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Obedience: for so work the honeybees,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Creatures that by a rule in nature teach

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The act of order to a peopled kingdom.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: They have a king and officers of sorts;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Where some, like magistrates, correct at home,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Others, like merchants, venture trade abroad,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Others, like soldiers, armed in their stings,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Make boot upon the summer’s velvet buds,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Which pillage they with merry march bring home

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To the tent-royal of their emperor;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Who, busied in his majesty, surveys

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The singing masons building roofs of gold,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The civil citizens kneading up the honey,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The poor mechanic porters crowding in

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Their heavy burdens at his narrow gate,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The sad-eyed justice, with his surly hum,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Delivering o’er to executors pale

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The lazy yawning drone. I this infer,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: That many things, having full reference

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: To one consent, may work contrariously:

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As many arrows, loosed several ways,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Come to one mark; as many ways meet in one town;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As many fresh streams meet in one salt sea;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: As many lines close in the dial’s centre;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: So many a thousand actions, once afoot,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: End in one purpose, and be all well borne

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Without defeat. Therefore to France, my liege.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Divide your happy England into four;

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Whereof take you one quarter into France,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: And you withal shall make all Gallia shake.

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: If we, with thrice such powers left at home,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Cannot defend our own doors from the dog,

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: Let us be worried and our nation lose

lines.speaker: CANTERBURY
lines.text: The name of hardiness and policy.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Call in the messengers sent from the Dauphin.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now are we well resolved; and, by God’s help,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And yours, the noble sinews of our power,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: France being ours, we’ll bend it to our awe,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or break it all to pieces: or there we’ll sit,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Ruling in large and ample empery

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O’er France and all her almost kingly dukedoms,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or lay these bones in an unworthy urn,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tombless, with no remembrance over them:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Either our history shall with full mouth

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Speak freely of our acts, or else our grave,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Like Turkish mute, shall have a tongueless mouth,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Not worshipp’d with a waxen epitaph.

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: May’t please your majesty to give us leave

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Freely to render what we have in charge;

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Or shall we sparingly show you far off

lines.speaker: FIRST AMBASSADOR
lines.text: The Dauphin’s meaning and our embassy?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We are no tyrant, but a Christian king;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Unto whose grace our passion is as subject

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As are our wretches fetter’d in our prisons:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore with frank and with uncurbed plainness

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tell us the Dauphin’s mind.

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Thus, then, in few.

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Your highness, lately sending into France,

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Did claim some certain dukedoms, in the right

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Of your great predecessor, King Edward the Third.

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: In answer of which claim, the prince our master

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Says that you savour too much of your youth,

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: And bids you be advised there’s nought in France

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: That can be with a nimble galliard won;

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: You cannot revel into dukedoms there.

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: He therefore sends you, meeter for your spirit,

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: This tun of treasure; and, in lieu of this,

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Desires you let the dukedoms that you claim

lines.speaker: AMBASSADOR
lines.text: Hear no more of you. This the Dauphin speaks.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What treasure, uncle?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Tennis-balls, my liege.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We are glad the Dauphin is so pleasant with us;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: His present and your pains we thank you for:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: When we have match’d our rackets to these balls,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We will, in France, by God’s grace, play a set

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall strike his father’s crown into the hazard.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tell him he hath made a match with such a wrangler

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That all the courts of France will be disturb’d

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With chases. And we understand him well,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: How he comes o’er us with our wilder days,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Not measuring what use we made of them.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We never valued this poor seat of England;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And therefore, living hence, did give ourself

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To barbarous licence; as ’tis ever common

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That men are merriest when they are from home.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But tell the Dauphin I will keep my state,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Be like a king and show my sail of greatness

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: When I do rouse me in my throne of France:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For that I have laid by my majesty

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And plodded like a man for working-days,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But I will rise there with so full a glory

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That I will dazzle all the eyes of France,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Yea, strike the Dauphin blind to look on us.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And tell the pleasant prince this mock of his

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hath turn’d his balls to gun-stones; and his soul

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall stand sore charged for the wasteful vengeance

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That shall fly with them: for many a thousand widows

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall this his mock mock out of their dear husbands;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Mock mothers from their sons, mock castles down;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And some are yet ungotten and unborn

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That shall have cause to curse the Dauphin’s scorn.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But this lies all within the will of God,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To whom I do appeal; and in whose name

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Tell you the Dauphin I am coming on,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To venge me as I may and to put forth

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My rightful hand in a well-hallow’d cause.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: So get you hence in peace; and tell the Dauphin

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: His jest will savour but of shallow wit,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: When thousands weep more than did laugh at it.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Convey them with safe conduct. Fare you well.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: This was a merry message.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We hope to make the sender blush at it.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore, my lords, omit no happy hour

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That may give furtherance to our expedition;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For we have now no thought in us but France,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Save those to God, that run before our business.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore let our proportions for these wars

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Be soon collected and all things thought upon

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That may with reasonable swiftness add

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: More feathers to our wings; for, God before,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We’ll chide this Dauphin at his father’s door.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore let every man now task his thought,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That this fair action may on foot be brought.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Henry, Gloucester, Bedford, Exeter, Warwick, Westmoreland, and Attendants.
Enter the Archbishop of Canterbury and the Bishop of Ely.
Now are we well prepared to know the pleasure Of our fair cousin Dauphin; for we hear Your greeting is from him, not from the king.

glosses.phrase: Where is my gracious Lord of Canterbury
glosses.definition: Henry summons the Archbishop — the scene's legal consultation begins
fully informed; settled in mind
in accordance with law and conscience
Salic Law — the Frankish legal tradition used to bar royal inheritance through the female line, invoked here against Henry's claim
shape; misrepresent

glosses.phrase: Or nicely charge your understanding soul
glosses.definition: or subtly burden your conscience
pledge; stake
authoritative sanction
is quickly spent in short human lives
solemn appeal

glosses.phrase: In terram Salicam mulieres ne succedant
glosses.definition: Latin: 'Into the Salic land let no women succeed' — the legal tag at the heart of Canterbury's speech
legendary founding king of the Franks, to whom the Salic Law was fictitiously attributed

glosses.phrase: the floods of Sala and of Elbe
glosses.definition: the Saale and Elbe rivers — German waterways central to Canterbury's geography lesson
Charlemagne (742–814) — king of the Franks and Holy Roman Emperor
founder of the Capetian dynasty of French kings (d. 996)
took the throne unlawfully — Canterbury is systematically questioning French royal legitimacy
Louis X of France (r. 1314–1316) — Canterbury's test case for inherited female right
Pepin the Short — Charlemagne's father, another Carolingian claim cited
Old Testament citation (Numbers 27:8) permitting daughters to inherit in the absence of sons — Canterbury's biblical trump card
formal address to the King
mighty
English king (r. 1327–1377) whose French claim is the historical basis for Henry's
Edward of Woodstock, Edward III's eldest son — hero of Crécy (1346) and Poitiers (1356)
Scottish raider — Canterbury shifts to the problem of border defense
the Scots pictured as a weasel — a small predator that attacks while the lion is away
England as an eagle — imperial, predatory
Canterbury's proverb: when the cat's away, the mice will play — if the king goes to France, the Scots will invade
Exeter's famous extended simile of the commonwealth as a beehive — kingdom ordered like a hive
officials administering justice
manual laborers; common workmen
royal pavilion — here, the queen bee's central cell
title of the French crown prince (pronounced DOH-fan) — equivalent to the English Prince of Wales
a cask of valuables — the gift from the Dauphin, which turns out to be insulting
the Dauphin's mocking gift — Henry's youthful reputation reduced to a children's game
tennis term — a point remembered but not yet scored, awaiting a second stroke; Henry turns the Dauphin's taunt back on him
disputant; opponent
cannonballs — the tennis balls reimagined as weapons

glosses.phrase: dazzle all the eyes of France
glosses.definition: blind the French with English arms
widow them of their children by war
holy justification — Henry takes the Dauphin's mockery as moral license

annotations.title: The Salic Law speech — historical fact, satirical stagecraft
annotations.body: Canterbury's sixty-line disquisition on the Salic Law is drawn directly, and in places verbatim, from Holinshed's Chronicles, which quotes the speech that Archbishop Chichele (the historical Canterbury) delivered to Parliament in 1414. Historically, the Salic Law — a 6th-century Frankish rule of inheritance — was invoked by 14th-century French jurists to bar inheritance of the French crown through the female line and thus to deny Edward III's claim. Canterbury's counter-argument is that the Salic Law applied only to the German Salian region, not to France, and that in any event the French themselves had repeatedly inherited through the female line. Whether he is right or tendentious is a genuine scholarly question; the historical arguments were and are real. But Shakespeare stages the exposition satirically. Canterbury speaks for sixty lines of tangled dynastic geography — Pharamond, Pepin, Hugh Capet, Lewis the Tenth, Louis the Ninth — and by line 88 Henry has to press him: 'May I with right and conscience make this claim?' The absurd scholarly cascade enacts the process by which a war of conquest is equipped with legal justification: laboriously, in learned Latin and medieval chronology, under pressure from a king who wants a yes.
sources: Gary Taylor (ed.), The Oxford Shakespeare: Henry V (1982), introduction and 1.2 commentary
Jonathan Bate, The Genius of Shakespeare (1997)

annotations.title: Exeter's honeybee speech — the commonwealth as hive
annotations.body: Exeter's extended simile of the kingdom as a beehive — with its magistrates, merchants, sad-eyed executioners, and idle drones all ordered under the queen bee — is a set-piece of Elizabethan political theory in verse. The hive was a commonplace figure for the ordered commonwealth, drawn from Virgil's Georgics Book IV and transmitted through Renaissance emblem books and courtesy literature. Shakespeare's particular version works as an argument: a kingdom can sustain both domestic governance and foreign expedition simultaneously, because each order of subject has its own proper work. This answers Canterbury's earlier warning about the Scots invading if the army crosses to France. The simile matters because it is the play's fullest positive vision of political order — a kind of counter-argument to the mess and improvisation that every other scene shows. Shakespeare places it here, at the war's planning moment, as if to say: this is the ideal the expedition claims to embody. Whether the play endorses the ideal or merely displays it is, again, left open.
sources: Pauline Croft, King James (2003), on hive imagery in early modern political writing

annotations.title: The tennis-balls episode — a legendary insult
annotations.body: The Dauphin's mocking gift of tennis balls, and Henry's elaborate in-kind reply, is one of the play's most famous set-pieces. It is also on historically shaky ground. The story appears in several 15th-century chronicles, including the First English Life of Henry V (c. 1513–14) and a ballad tradition that predates the chronicles themselves. But it is not in the most contemporary French sources. Most modern historians treat it as legend — a crystallizing narrative device for a diplomatic insult whose actual form, if any, is lost. What Shakespeare does with the episode is, however, historically precise in another way: he turns Henry's answer into a prophetic catalogue of French suffering ('mock mothers from their sons, / Mock castles down…'), and he locates the conversion of the insult into a war-justification at the crown-coronation moment of the play. 'His jest shall savour but of shallow wit, / When thousands weep more than did laugh at it' (lines 296–297): Henry transforms personal insult into collective retribution. Readers should notice how comfortably the rhetoric of wounded royal honour does this work — and how the Dauphin is made, in effect, to authorize the war.
sources: The First English Life of King Henry the Fifth, ed. C. L. Kingsford (1911)
Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)
A. R. Humphreys, The Arden Shakespeare: Henry V (1968)

annotations.title: 'Well-hallow'd cause' — how a claim becomes a crusade
annotations.body: Henry's closing line — 'But this lies all within the will of God, / To whom I do appeal' — places the war under divine sanction. This is the characteristic rhetorical move of Henry's speeches throughout the play, and by this point in the scene we have watched it be manufactured: Canterbury's compromised legal opinion, the Church's institutional interest, the sixty lines of Salic genealogy, the lords' eager endorsement, and finally the Dauphin's tennis-balls giving Henry the moral licence to call it an affront. What begins in 1.1 as a bill against Church lands ends in 1.2 as a 'well-hallow'd cause' with God on its side. The play does not tell us that this is cynical; it merely stages the process with unusual clarity. The same providentialism will return after Agincourt, when Henry forbids any soldier to claim credit for the victory on pain of death. Readers may notice that the same rhetorical apparatus that sanctifies conquest in 1.2 will sanctify victory in 4.8.
sources: Norman Rabkin, 'Either/Or,' in Shakespeare and the Problem of Meaning (1981)

trials.prompt: What is the 'Salic Law' that Canterbury explicates at length?
options: A Frankish legal tradition cited by 14th-century French jurists to bar royal inheritance through the female line — which Canterbury argues does not apply to France
A tax on salt levied during Edward III's reign
A treaty that ended the Hundred Years' War in 1453
A ceremonial law requiring the king to be crowned at Rheims

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare stage Canterbury's Salic Law speech at such exhausting length?
options: Because the Elizabethan audience wanted dynastic trivia
Because Holinshed's Chronicles could not be abridged for legal reasons
The tangled scholarly exposition dramatizes the process by which a war of conquest is laboriously equipped with legal justification — the legalism is itself the satire
Because Canterbury is a bad speaker and Shakespeare wanted to show it

trials.prompt: Exeter's 'honey-bees' speech (lines 184–220) uses the beehive as a metaphor for —
options: The Elizabethan court's intrigues
The ordered commonwealth — a kingdom in which magistrates, merchants, and laborers each have their proper work, an image drawn from Virgil's Georgics
The Roman Catholic Church
The guild system of London

trials.prompt: The Dauphin's gift of tennis balls is —
options: A well-documented historical event of 1414
A legendary insult — attested in some 15th-century chronicles but not in the contemporary French sources; Shakespeare inherited it from tradition
Shakespeare's own invention, without precedent
An actual diplomatic document preserved in the British Library

trials.prompt: What rhetorical transformation does Henry perform in his reply to the tennis balls (lines 272–300)?
options: He forgives the insult and offers the Dauphin peace
He converts a personal jest into a prophecy of mass French suffering, using the Dauphin's mockery to license collective retribution
He challenges the Dauphin to a game of tennis
He ignores the insult and goes to bed

trials.prompt: By the end of 1.2, how has the motive for war been framed?
options: As pure self-defense against an imminent French invasion
As a 'well-hallow'd cause' under divine sanction — the scene has staged the process by which a dynastic claim, a compromised legal opinion, and a personal insult combine into a war blessed by God
As a trade dispute over cloth exports
As a response to the killing of an English ambassador
